verses.flow: A revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave to him, to show to his servants the things which must come to pass in swiftness, and he signified it, having sent it through his angel to his servant John,
verses.note: GREEK: "Apokalypsis" (ἀποκάλυψις, Strong's G602) means an unveiling or uncovering — not destruction, but disclosure of hidden realities. "Esēmanen" (ἐσήμανεν, from σημαίνω, G4591) means "he signified," indicating the vision was communicated through signs and symbols, not bare propositional statements. This frames the entire book as symbolic-visionary communication mediated through an angelic chain: God → Christ → angel → John → servants.

verses.flow: who bore witness to the word of God and to the testimony of Jesus Christ — as many things as he saw.

verses.flow: Blessed is the one reading and those hearing the words of the prophecy and keeping the things written in it, for the time is near.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This is the first of seven beatitudes in Revelation (cf. 14:13; 16:15; 19:9; 20:6; 22:7; 22:14). "The one reading" (ὁ ἀναγινώσκων) is singular, referring to the public reader in the assembly, while "those hearing" (οἱ ἀκούοντες) is plural — reflecting the liturgical practice of one reader proclaiming aloud to a gathered congregation. GREEK: "Kairos" (καιρός, G2540) denotes an appointed, decisive season — not mere chronological time (chronos), but the critical moment of divine fulfillment.

verses.flow: John, to the seven assemblies that are in Asia: Grace to you and peace from the One who is and who was and who is coming, and from the seven spirits that are before his throne,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The tripartite greeting "from the One who is and who was and who is coming" is a dynamic expansion of the divine name revealed in Exodus 3:14 (LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν). The unusual Greek here — "ὁ ἦν" uses a finite verb as a substantive in violation of normal grammar — signals that this title transcends ordinary language. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "seven spirits before his throne" (ἑπτὰ πνευμάτων) likely draws on the imagery of the sevenfold Spirit of God (cf. Isa 11:2; Zech 4:2-10), representing the fullness of the Holy Spirit in his heavenly court function, or possibly the seven angelic presences before God (cf. Tobit 12:15).

verses.flow: and from Jesus Christ, the faithful witness, the firstborn of the dead and the ruler of the kings of the earth. To the one loving us and having loosed us from our sins by his blood —
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Three Christological titles form a climax: "faithful witness" (ὁ μάρτυς ὁ πιστός) — Christ's prophetic office; "firstborn of the dead" (ὁ πρωτότοκος τῶν νεκρῶν) — his resurrection priority and supremacy (cf. Col 1:18); "ruler of the kings of the earth" (ὁ ἄρχων τῶν βασιλέων τῆς γῆς) — his royal dominion, echoing Psalm 89:27 (LXX 88:28). TYPOLOGY: "Firstborn" (πρωτότοκος, G4416) carries both priority and sovereignty, recalling Israel as God's firstborn (Exod 4:22) now fulfilled in the risen Christ. "Lysanti" (λύσαντι) — "having loosed" — some manuscripts read "lousanti" (having washed), but the better attested reading emphasizes liberation from sin's bondage.

verses.flow: and he made us a kingdom, priests to his God and Father — to him be the glory and the dominion unto the ages of the ages. Amen.
Behold, he comes with the clouds, and every eye shall see him, and those who pierced him, and all the tribes of the earth shall mourn over him. Yes, amen.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse fuses Daniel 7:13 (one coming with the clouds) and Zechariah 12:10 (they shall look upon the one whom they pierced). The mourning of "all the tribes of the earth" echoes Zechariah 12:10-14, where each tribe mourns separately. "Exekentēsan" (ἐξεκέντησαν, G1574) — "pierced" — is the same verb used in John 19:37, linking the crucifixion directly to this eschatological appearing. This establishes the interpretive key: the crucified one is the coming one.

verses.flow: "I am the Alpha and the Omega," says the Lord God, the One who is and who was and who is coming, the Almighty.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Alpha and Omega" — the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet — declares absolute divine sovereignty over all of history from beginning to end. This title is spoken by "the Lord God" (κύριος ὁ θεός), and yet in verse 17 the same kind of title ("the First and the Last") is claimed by the risen Christ, establishing a profound identification. GREEK: "Pantokratōr" (παντοκράτωρ, G3841) — "the Almighty" or "the All-Ruling One" — appears nine times in Revelation and translates the Hebrew "YHWH Tseva'ot" (Lord of Hosts) in the LXX, carrying divine council overtones of cosmic military sovereignty.

verses.flow: I, John, your brother and fellow-partaker in the tribulation and kingdom and patient endurance in Jesus, came to be on the island called Patmos on account of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus.
I came to be in the Spirit on the Lord's day, and I heard behind me a great voice as of a trumpet,

verses.flow: saying, "What you see, write into a book and send it to the seven assemblies: to Ephesus and to Smyrna and to Pergamos and to Thyatira and to Sardis and to Philadelphia and to Laodicea."

verses.flow: And I turned to see the voice that was speaking with me, and having turned, I saw seven golden lampstands,

verses.flow: and in the midst of the lampstands one like a son of man, having been clothed in a garment reaching to the feet and having been girded about at the breasts with a golden sash.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "One like a son of man" (ὅμοιον υἱὸν ἀνθρώπου) directly echoes Daniel 7:13 (LXX), where the son of man figure receives everlasting dominion from the Ancient of Days. Yet the description that follows — white hair, eyes of flame — borrows imagery from the Ancient of Days himself (Dan 7:9), collapsing the distinction between the two figures and affirming Christ's full divine identity. GREEK: "Podērē" (ποδήρη, G4158) — a garment reaching to the feet — is a hapax legomenon in the NT. In the LXX it describes the high priestly robe (Exod 28:4; Ezek 9:2), signifying Christ's priestly office amid the lampstands of his assemblies.

verses.flow: And his head and his hairs were white as white wool, as snow, and his eyes were as a flame of fire,

words.m: verb perfect passive participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: and his feet were like fine bronze, as having been refined in a furnace, and his voice was as the voice of many waters,
verses.note: GREEK: "Chalkolibanō" (χαλκολιβάνῳ, G5474) is an extremely rare word, likely a hapax legomenon outside Revelation (also 2:18), referring to a brilliant bronze or orichalcum alloy. Its exact composition is debated; it conveys incandescent, refined brilliance suggesting both judgment and purity. TYPOLOGY: "His voice as the voice of many waters" echoes Ezekiel 43:2, where the glory of the God of Israel returns to the temple with a sound like many waters — again identifying the risen Christ with the God of Israel.

verses.flow: and having in his right hand seven stars, and out of his mouth a sharp two-edged sword proceeding, and his countenance was as the sun shines in its power.

verses.flow: And when I saw him, I fell at his feet as dead, and he placed his right hand upon me, saying, "Fear not. I am the First and the Last,

verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and the Living One — and I became dead, and behold, I am living unto the ages of the ages — and I hold the keys of death and of Hades.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "I became dead and behold I am living unto the ages of the ages" is a creedal proclamation of bodily resurrection and eternal life. The juxtaposition of historical death ("egenomēn nekros" — aorist, punctiliar past event) with ongoing life ("zōn eimi" — present participle with present verb) emphasizes the permanent victory of resurrection. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Holding "the keys of death and of Hades" (τὰς κλεῖς τοῦ θανάτου καὶ τοῦ ᾅδου) declares Christ's authority over the realm of the dead — a domain previously under other powers (cf. Heb 2:14-15). "Hades" (ᾅδης, G86) translates Hebrew Sheol, the abode of the dead.

verses.flow: Write therefore the things which you saw, and the things which are, and the things which are about to come to pass after these things.

The mystery of the seven stars which you saw upon my right hand, and the seven golden lampstands: the seven stars are angels of the seven assemblies, and the seven lampstands are the seven assemblies."
verses.note: GREEK: "Mystērion" (μυστήριον, G3466) denotes a previously hidden divine truth now revealed — not a puzzle to be solved, but a reality disclosed by God. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The identification of the seven stars as "angels" (ἄγγελοι) of the seven assemblies is debated: they may be heavenly guardians assigned to each congregation (reflecting the concept of patron angels over communities, cf. Dan 10:13, 20-21; 12:1), or human messengers/overseers. The divine council framework favors celestial beings who represent or mediate for each assembly before God's throne. The lampstands as assemblies recalls Zechariah 4, where the lampstand represents God's covenant people sustained by his Spirit.

verses.flow: To the angel of the assembly in Ephesus write: These things says the One holding the seven stars in His right hand, the One walking in the midst of the seven golden lampstands:
verses.note: GREEK: "Angelos" (ἄγγελος, Strong's G32) may denote a heavenly messenger assigned to the assembly or the human overseer/bishop of the congregation. "Kratōn" (κρατῶν, G2902) means holding with sovereign strength, not merely carrying. The imagery of Christ walking among the lampstands echoes His priestly role among His people. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Christ is depicted as the sovereign Lord moving among His assemblies as God walked in Eden (Gen 3:8) and dwelt among Israel in the tabernacle.

verses.flow: I know your works, and your labor, and your endurance, and that you are not able to bear evil ones, and you tested those calling themselves apostles and they are not, and you found them false;
and you have endurance, and you have borne on account of My name, and you have not grown weary.
But I have against you, that you have left your first love.
verses.note: GREEK: "Agapēn tēn prōtēn" (ἀγάπην τὴν πρώτην)—the first love. "Aphēkes" (ἀφῆκες, from ἀφίημι, G863) means to release, abandon, or forsake, indicating a decisive departure rather than gradual drift. THEOLOGICAL: Right doctrine without fervent love is insufficient before Christ the Judge; orthodoxy must be animated by covenant devotion.

verses.flow: Remember therefore from where you have fallen, and repent, and do the first works. But if not, I am coming to you, and I will remove your lampstand from its place, unless you repent.
But this you have, that you hate the works of the Nicolaitans, which I also hate.

verses.flow: The one having an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the assemblies. To the one who overcomes, I will give to him to eat from the tree of life, which is in the Paradise of God.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "tree of life" (ξύλον τῆς ζωῆς) directly recalls Genesis 2:9 and 3:22–24, now restored in the eschatological Paradise of God (Rev 22:2,14). What was lost through the first Adam's failure is granted to those who overcome through the last Adam. GREEK: "Nikōnti" (νικῶντι, G3528) is a present active participle—the one who is continually overcoming, not a one-time act.

verses.flow: And to the angel of the assembly in Smyrna write: These things says the First and the Last, who became dead and lived:

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: I know your tribulation and your poverty—but you are rich—and the blasphemy from those saying themselves to be Jews, and they are not, but are a synagogue of Satan.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Those claiming to be Jews but constituting a "synagogue of Satan" (συναγωγὴν τοῦ Σατανᾶ) are ethnic Jews who reject Christ as Messiah and persecute His assembly. Paul makes a similar distinction between outward and inward Israel (Rom 2:28–29; 9:6). HISTORICAL: Smyrna had a large Jewish community known for hostility toward early Christians, later documented in the martyrdom of Polycarp (c. AD 155).

verses.flow: Fear nothing of what you are about to suffer. Behold, the devil is about to cast some of you into prison, that you may be tested, and you will have tribulation ten days. Be faithful unto death, and I will give to you the crown of life.
verses.note: GREEK: "Stephanos" (στέφανος, G4735) is the victor's wreath, not the royal diadem (diádēma); it connotes reward for faithful endurance in contest. "Achri thanatou" (ἄχρι θανάτου) means unto the point of death—faithfulness to the uttermost. THEOLOGICAL: The promise of the crown of life parallels James 1:12 and indicates eschatological reward for those who endure persecution.

verses.flow: The one having an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the assemblies. The one who overcomes shall by no means be harmed by the second death.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "second death" (θανάτου τοῦ δευτέρου) is defined in Rev 20:14 as the lake of fire. The double negative "ou mē" (οὐ μή) is the strongest negation possible in Greek—absolute assurance that the overcomer will never be touched by final judgment.

verses.flow: And to the angel of the assembly in Pergamum write: These things says the One having the sharp two-edged sword:

I know where you dwell, where the throne of Satan is, and you hold fast My name, and you did not deny My faith, even in the days of Antipas, My faithful witness, who was killed among you, where Satan dwells.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Pergamum was the center of imperial cult worship in Asia Minor, housing a massive altar to Zeus (possibly "the throne of Satan") and being the first city in Asia to build a temple to Augustus (29 BC). GREEK: "Antipas" (Ἀντιπᾶς) is a contracted form of Antipatros; he is called "ho martys mou ho pistos" (ὁ μάρτυς μου ὁ πιστός), using the same title applied to Christ Himself in Rev 1:5.

verses.flow: The one having an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the assemblies. To the one who overcomes, I will give to him of the hidden manna, and I will give to him a white stone, and upon the stone a new name written, which no one knows except the one receiving it.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "hidden manna" recalls the manna preserved in the Ark of the Covenant (Exod 16:33–34; Heb 9:4) and points to Christ as the true bread from heaven (John 6:31–35,49–51). GREEK: "Psēphon leukēn" (ψῆφον λευκήν)—a white stone (G5586). In Roman judicial practice a white stone signified acquittal; in social custom it served as an admission token to banquets. The "new name" (ónoma kainón) suggests a new identity and intimate relationship known only between Christ and the overcomer.

verses.flow: And to the angel of the assembly in Thyatira write: These things says the Son of God, the One having His eyes like a flame of fire, and His feet like fine bronze:
I know your works, and your love, and your faith, and your service, and your endurance, and your last works are greater than the first.

verses.flow: But I have against you, that you permit the woman Jezebel, the one calling herself a prophetess, and she teaches and deceives My servants to commit fornication and to eat things sacrificed to idols.
And I gave her time that she might repent, and she does not will to repent of her fornication.

verses.flow: Behold, I am casting her into a bed, and those committing adultery with her into great tribulation, unless they repent of her works.

verses.flow: And her children I will kill with death, and all the assemblies shall know that I am the One searching minds and hearts; and I will give to each of you according to your works.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "I am the One searching minds and hearts" (ὁ ἐραυνῶν νεφροὺς καὶ καρδίας) applies to Christ the divine prerogative ascribed to YHWH in Jer 17:10 and Ps 7:9. "Nephroi" (νεφροί, G3510) literally means kidneys/reins, representing the seat of inner motives in Hebraic thought. This is a direct claim to deity and omniscience by the risen Christ.

verses.flow: But to you I say, to the rest in Thyatira, as many as do not have this teaching, who have not known the deep things of Satan, as they say—I cast upon you no other burden.
Nevertheless, what you have, hold fast until I come.

verses.flow: And the one who overcomes and the one keeping My works until the end, I will give to him authority over the nations,
and he shall shepherd them with a rod of iron, as the vessels of the potter are shattered,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This is a direct quotation of Psalm 2:9 (LXX), a royal messianic psalm. Christ shares His messianic authority over the nations with those who overcome, fulfilling the promise that believers are co-heirs and co-rulers with Him (Rom 8:17; 2 Tim 2:12). GREEK: "Poimanei" (ποιμανεῖ, G4165) means to shepherd or rule; "rhabdō sidēra" (ῥάβδῳ σιδηρᾷ) is a rod of iron—an instrument of absolute sovereign authority.

verses.flow: even as I have received from My Father; and I will give to him the morning star.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "morning star" (τὸν ἀστέρα τὸν πρωϊνόν) connects to Num 24:17 ("a star shall come out of Jacob") and to Christ's self-identification in Rev 22:16 as "the bright morning star." Christ gives Himself—His own risen glory and kingdom authority—to the overcomer. THEOLOGICAL: This gift represents participation in the dawn of the eschatological age, sharing in Christ's own radiant dominion.

verses.flow: And to the angel of the assembly in Sardis write: These things says the One having the seven Spirits of God and the seven stars: I know your works, that you have a name that you live, and you are dead.
verses.note: GREEK: "The seven Spirits of God" (τὰ ἑπτὰ πνεύματα τοῦ θεοῦ) — pneûma (G4151) here likely references the sevenfold Spirit of God (cf. Isa 11:2; Rev 1:4; 4:5; 5:6), denoting the fullness of the Holy Spirit in his operative completeness. THEOLOGICAL: The paradox "you have a name that you live, and you are dead" exposes a church with outward reputation but inward spiritual death — a solemn warning against nominal faith.

verses.flow: Become watchful, and strengthen the things remaining, which were about to die; for I have not found your works completed before my God.
Remember therefore how you have received and heard, and keep it, and repent. If therefore you do not watch, I will come as a thief, and you will by no means know what hour I will come upon you.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Christ's coming "as a thief" (ὡς κλέπτης) recurs as a major eschatological motif (Matt 24:43; 1 Thess 5:2; 2 Pet 3:10; Rev 16:15). The unexpectedness of judgment is the point of comparison, not the moral character of the act. GREEK: μνημόνευε (present imperative) — "keep on remembering"; the tense implies continuous, habitual recollection of the apostolic tradition received (εἴληφας, perfect tense — indicating abiding effect).

verses.flow: But you have a few names in Sardis which have not defiled their garments, and they shall walk with me in white, for they are worthy.

verses.flow: The one who conquers shall thus be clothed in white garments, and I will by no means blot out his name from the book of life, and I will confess his name before my Father and before his angels.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Three promises to the overcomer: (1) white garments of purity and victory, (2) preservation of the name in the book of life (cf. Exod 32:32-33; Dan 12:1; Phil 4:3), and (3) personal confession by Christ before the Father and his angels (cf. Matt 10:32; Luke 12:8). DIVINE_COUNCIL: Christ confessing the believer's name "before his angels" (ἐνώπιον τῶν ἀγγέλων αὐτοῦ) portrays the heavenly court scene where the divine council witnesses Christ's advocacy.

verses.flow: And to the angel of the assembly in Philadelphia write: These things says the Holy One, the True One, the One having the key of David, the One who opens and no one shall shut, and who shuts and no one opens:
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "The key of David" (τὴν κλεῖν Δαυίδ) directly echoes Isaiah 22:22 (LXX), where Eliakim receives authority over the house of David. Christ claims this Davidic stewardship as the messianic King with absolute authority to grant or deny access to the kingdom. GREEK: kleís (G2807) — "key," symbolizing sovereign jurisdiction. The fourfold description — Holy, True, key-holder, opener/shutter — presents Christ in unmistakable royal-priestly authority.

verses.flow: I know your works — behold, I have set before you an open door which no one is able to shut — that you have little power, and you have kept my word, and have not denied my name.

verses.flow: Behold, I give those from the synagogue of Satan, those saying themselves to be Jews and are not, but lie — behold, I will make them so that they will come and will worship before your feet, and they will know that I have loved you.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "The synagogue of Satan" (τῆς συναγωγῆς τοῦ Σατανᾶ) refers to Jewish opponents in Philadelphia who claimed covenant identity but persecuted the believing community. TYPOLOGY: The promise that adversaries will "worship before your feet" echoes Isaiah 60:14 and 49:23, where Gentile nations bow before restored Israel — here reversed, as those who falsely claim to be Israel bow before the true people of God, the assembly of Christ.

verses.flow: Because you have kept the word of my endurance, I also will keep you from the hour of trial, the one about to come upon the whole inhabited earth, to try those who dwell upon the earth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The word of my endurance" (τὸν λόγον τῆς ὑπομονῆς μου) — the genitive may be subjective (the endurance Christ himself displayed) or objective (the message that calls for endurance). The promise to "keep from" (τηρήσω ἐκ) the hour of trial is debated: ἐκ may indicate preservation through or removal from the coming worldwide tribulation. GREEK: οἰκουμένης (G3625) — the "inhabited earth," a term used in Roman imperial contexts for the known civilized world.

verses.flow: I am coming quickly. Hold fast what you have, so that no one may take your crown.

verses.flow: The one who conquers — I will make him a pillar in the temple of my God, and he shall by no means go out from it anymore, and I will write upon him the name of my God, and the name of the city of my God, the new Jerusalem, which comes down out of heaven from my God, and my new name.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The threefold name-inscription — God's name, the new Jerusalem's name, and Christ's new name — signifies total belonging, citizenship, and intimate identification with Christ. TYPOLOGY: The "pillar in the temple" (στῦλον ἐν τῷ ναῷ) evokes the permanence of Jachin and Boaz in Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 7:21), yet surpasses them: this pillar "shall by no means go out anymore." The new Jerusalem "coming down out of heaven" anticipates the consummation in Revelation 21:2.

verses.flow: The one having an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the assemblies.
And to the angel of the assembly in Laodicea write: These things says the Amen, the faithful and true Witness, the Beginning of the creation of God:
verses.note: GREEK: "The Amen" (ὁ Ἀμήν) — Christ as a personal title, unique in the NT, echoing Isaiah 65:16 LXX where God is called "the God of truth (amen)." THEOLOGICAL: "The Beginning (ἀρχή, G746) of the creation of God" does not denote Christ as the first creature but as the originating source and sovereign ruler of creation (cf. Col 1:15-18; John 1:3). The term ἀρχή carries the sense of origin, first cause, and governing principle.

verses.flow: I know your works, that you are neither cold nor hot. Would that you were cold or hot!
So, because you are lukewarm, and neither hot nor cold, I am about to vomit you out of my mouth.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Laodicea was served by an aqueduct from hot springs near Hierapolis; by the time the water arrived, it was notoriously lukewarm and mineral-laden, often inducing nausea. Cold water from nearby Colossae was refreshing. The metaphor would have been immediately visceral to the original hearers. GREEK: ἐμέσαι (G1692) — "to vomit, spew out," a hapax legomenon in the NT, expressing intense revulsion.

verses.flow: Because you say, "I am rich, and have become wealthy, and have need of nothing," and you do not know that you are the wretched one, and pitiable, and poor, and blind, and naked —

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: I counsel you to buy from me gold refined by fire, so that you may become rich, and white garments, so that you may clothe yourself and the shame of your nakedness may not be made manifest, and eye-salve to anoint your eyes, so that you may see.
I, as many as I love, I rebuke and discipline. Be zealous therefore, and repent.
verses.note: GREEK: φιλέω (G5368) — "I love" with personal affection, distinct from ἀγαπάω; Christ's rebuke is grounded in intimate, familial love. TYPOLOGY: The pattern of divine discipline as evidence of sonship echoes Proverbs 3:11-12 and Hebrews 12:5-6. ζήλευε (present imperative) — "be continually zealous," calling for sustained, burning commitment rather than a single act.

verses.flow: Behold, I stand at the door and knock. If anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in to him and will dine with him, and he with me.

TYPOLOGY: This sets the stage for the complementary hymn of Rev 5:9-10, where the Lamb is declared worthy on account of redemption, together forming a complete theology of God's sovereign work in creation and salvation.

DIVINE_COUNCIL: The seven horns signify complete power, and the seven eyes — identified as the seven Spirits of God sent into all the earth — echo Zech 4:10 and Isa 11:2, depicting the Lamb's omniscience and omnipresent sovereign agency through the Spirit. TYPOLOGY: The slain Lamb evokes the Passover lamb (Exod 12:3-6) and the Suffering Servant of Isa 53:7, both fulfilled in Christ's sacrificial death.

verses.note: GREEK: "Choinix" (χοῖνιξ, G5518) was a dry measure of approximately one quart, roughly a day's ration for one person. "Denarion" (δηνάριον, G1220) was a standard day's wage for a laborer (Matt 20:2). HISTORICAL: This depicts severe inflation — a day's wage buying food for only one person — while luxury goods (oil and wine) remain protected, suggesting economic disparity under famine conditions. The command "do not harm" (μὴ ἀδικήσῃς) implies divine restraint upon the judgment.

verses.note: GREEK: "Chloros" (χλωρός, G5515) means "pale green" or "yellowish-green," the color of a corpse — hence translated "pale." This is the only horseman explicitly named. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Authority (exousia) is "given" (edothē, divine passive), indicating these agents operate under delegated heavenly authority.

verses.flow: saying, "Amen! The blessing and the glory and the wisdom and the thanksgiving and the honor and the power and the might be to our God unto the ages of the ages. Amen."

verses.flow: And one of the elders answered, saying to me, "These who are clothed in the white robes—who are they, and from where have they come?"

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And I said to him, "My lord, you know." And he said to me, "These are the ones coming out of the great tribulation, and they washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The paradox of robes made white by blood is central to atonement theology—purity comes through the sacrificial death of the Lamb (cf. Isa 1:18; 1 John 1:7; Heb 9:14). GREEK: τῆς θλίψεως τῆς μεγάλης (tēs thlipseōs tēs megalēs) is "the tribulation, the great one"—the double article emphasizes a specific, climactic period of suffering. HISTORICAL: θλῖψις (thlipsis, G2347) denotes crushing pressure; early Christians understood persecution as participation in the Messiah's sufferings (Col 1:24).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Because of this they are before the throne of God, and they serve Him day and night in His temple; and the One sitting upon the throne shall spread His tabernacle over them.
verses.note: GREEK: σκηνώσει (skēnōsei, G4637) means "shall tabernacle" or "shall spread His tent"—the same root used in John 1:14 where the Word "tabernacled" among us. TYPOLOGY: God dwelling among His people fulfills the tabernacle/temple trajectory from Exodus 25:8 through Ezekiel 37:27 to its consummation here. The priestly language of λατρεύουσιν (latreuousin, "they render sacred service") indicates the redeemed function as a kingdom of priests (Rev 1:6; 5:10).

verses.flow: They shall hunger no more, neither shall they thirst any more; neither shall the sun fall upon them, nor any scorching heat.
For the Lamb, the One in the midst of the throne, shall shepherd them, and shall guide them unto springs of waters of life; and God shall wipe away every tear from their eyes."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The Lamb as shepherd is a deliberate paradox—the sacrificial victim becomes the ruling shepherd, fulfilling Psalm 23, Isaiah 40:11, and Ezekiel 34:23 where God Himself and His Davidic servant shepherd the flock. GREEK: ποιμανεῖ (poimanei, G4165) means "shall shepherd" or "shall tend as a shepherd"—encompassing feeding, guiding, and protecting. The wiping of tears echoes Isaiah 25:8, the eschatological promise of death's defeat and sorrow's end, brought to final consummation in Revelation 21:4.

verses.flow: And when He opened the seventh seal, there came about a silence in heaven for about half an hour.
verses.note: GREEK: The word sigē (σιγή, Strong's G4602) denotes a profound, awe-struck silence — not merely the absence of sound but a deliberate, reverential hush. This is its only occurrence in Revelation. THEOLOGICAL: After the ceaseless worship described in chapters 4-5, this silence is dramatically significant. It signals the gravity of what follows — the full outpouring of God's judgments. Some scholars connect this to Habakkuk 2:20: "The Lord is in His holy temple; let all the earth keep silence before Him." DIVINE_COUNCIL: The entire heavenly court — angels, living creatures, elders — ceases all activity, indicating that the divine council itself stands in solemn anticipation of the decree about to be executed.

verses.flow: And I saw the seven angels who stand before God, and there were given to them seven trumpets.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And another angel came and stood at the altar, having a golden censer, and there was given to him much incense, that he might add it to the prayers of all the saints upon the golden altar which is before the throne.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The angel's priestly ministry at the golden altar directly echoes the Levitical incense offering (Exod 30:1-8). The golden censer (libanōtós, λιβανωτός, Strong's G3031) and the mingling of incense with the prayers of the saints recalls Psalm 141:2: "Let my prayer be set before You as incense." THEOLOGICAL: The saints' prayers are not lost or forgotten; they are gathered, sanctified, and presented before the very throne of God. This scene reveals that the prayers of God's people are integral to the unfolding of His eschatological justice.

verses.flow: And the smoke of the incense ascended with the prayers of the saints out of the hand of the angel before God.
And the angel took the censer and filled it from the fire of the altar and cast it unto the earth; and there came thunders and voices and lightnings and an earthquake.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The same altar and fire that carried the saints' prayers upward now becomes the instrument of judgment cast downward. The sequence — prayers ascend, fire descends — establishes a direct causal link between the intercession of the saints and the judgments of God upon the earth. TYPOLOGY: The thunders, voices, lightnings, and earthquake recall the theophany at Sinai (Exod 19:16-18), marking this as a covenant-enforcement action by the God of Israel. STRUCTURE: This verse serves as a hinge between the intercession scene (vv. 3-4) and the trumpet judgments (vv. 6-13).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the seven angels having the seven trumpets prepared themselves that they might sound.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: And the first sounded, and there came hail and fire mingled with blood, and it was cast unto the earth; and the third of the earth was burned up, and the third of the trees was burned up, and all green grass was burned up.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The first trumpet directly echoes the seventh plague upon Egypt — hail mingled with fire and blood (Exod 9:23-25). The Exodus plague typology pervades the trumpet sequence, presenting these judgments as a new and greater Exodus. The fraction "the third" (τὸ τρίτον) indicates partial yet severe judgment — divine restraint before the final consummation. GREEK: katekáē (κατεκάη) is the aorist passive of katakaiō (Strong's G2618), meaning "was utterly consumed by burning."

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And the second angel sounded, and something as a great mountain burning with fire was cast into the sea; and the third of the sea became blood,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: and the third of the creatures in the sea, those having life, died; and the third of the ships were destroyed.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the third angel sounded, and there fell out of heaven a great star, burning as a torch, and it fell upon the third of the rivers and upon the springs of the waters.
And the name of the star is called Wormwood; and the third of the waters became wormwood, and many of the men died from the waters, because they were made bitter.
verses.note: GREEK: Ápsinthos (Ἄψινθος, Strong's G894) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, referring to the plant Artemisia absinthium, known for its extreme bitterness. It was proverbially associated with sorrow and divine judgment. TYPOLOGY: The poisoning of waters echoes the bitter waters of Marah (Exod 15:23) and especially the prophetic curse of Jeremiah 9:15 and 23:15, where God declares He will feed faithless Israel wormwood and give them poisoned water to drink. The star's naming is significant — in Revelation, naming reveals essential nature and divine purpose.

verses.flow: And the fourth angel sounded, and the third of the sun was struck, and the third of the moon, and the third of the stars, so that the third of them was darkened, and the day did not shine for the third of it, and the night likewise.

verses.flow: And I looked, and I heard one eagle flying in mid-heaven, saying with a great voice, "Woe, woe, woe to those dwelling upon the earth, because of the remaining voices of the trumpet of the three angels who are about to sound!"
verses.note: GREEK: The word mesouranēma (μεσουράνημα, Strong's G3321) refers to the zenith of the sky — mid-heaven — the point of maximum visibility, ensuring the warning is inescapable. This rare word occurs only in Revelation (8:13; 14:6; 19:17). STRUCTURE: This verse serves as a dramatic interlude and literary hinge between the first four trumpets (affecting nature) and the final three, now designated as "woes" (ouaí, οὐαί), which will afflict humanity directly. The eagle — some manuscripts read aetós (eagle) rather than ángelos (angel) — may evoke Deuteronomy 28:49, where an eagle symbolizes the swiftness of coming judgment. THEOLOGICAL: The threefold "woe" corresponds to the three remaining trumpets and intensifies the dread — what has come is terrible, but what remains will be far worse for those dwelling upon the earth.

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the fifth angel sounded, and I saw a star having fallen out of heaven unto the earth, and there was given to him the key of the shaft of the abyss.
verses.note: GREEK: "Star having fallen" (ἀστέρα πεπτωκότα, astéra peptōkóta) uses a perfect active participle, indicating a completed prior fall — the star had already fallen before the trumpet sounded. In apocalyptic literature, "stars" frequently represent angelic or divine council beings (cf. Isa 14:12; Job 38:7). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The giving of the "key" (κλείς, kleís, Strong's G2807) implies delegated authority — this fallen being acts only within divinely permitted boundaries. The "abyss" (ἄβυσσος, ábyssos, Strong's G12) denotes the cosmic prison of bound spiritual entities (cf. Luke 8:31; 2 Pet 2:4; Jude 6).

verses.flow: And he opened the shaft of the abyss, and smoke went up out of the shaft as smoke of a great furnace, and the sun was darkened, and the air, from the smoke of the shaft.
And out of the smoke came forth locusts upon the earth, and authority was given to them, as the scorpions of the earth have authority.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The locust plague deliberately echoes the eighth plague of Egypt (Exod 10:12-15), yet these are no ordinary locusts — they are given scorpion-like authority (ἐξουσία, exousía), marking them as agents of supernatural torment. GREEK: ἀκρίδες (akrídes, Strong's G200) are literal locusts, but the description that follows transforms them into hybrid demonic entities. Joel 1-2 provides the primary OT background, where a locust army serves as a vehicle for divine judgment.

verses.flow: And it was said to them that they should not harm the grass of the earth, nor any green thing, nor any tree, but only the men who do not have the seal of God upon their foreheads.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The restriction against harming vegetation inverts the nature of locusts entirely, confirming their supernatural character. The "seal of God" (σφραγῖδα τοῦ θεοῦ) upon the foreheads connects directly to the sealing of the 144,000 in Rev 7:3-4 and echoes the protective mark of Ezekiel 9:4-6. God's people are distinguished and preserved amidst cosmic judgment.

verses.flow: And it was given to them that they should not kill them, but that they should be tormented five months; and their torment was as the torment of a scorpion when it strikes a man.
And in those days men shall seek death and shall by no means find it, and they shall desire to die, and death flees from them.
verses.note: GREEK: The phrase οὐ μὴ εὑρήσουσιν (ou mē heurēsousin) is the strongest form of negation in Greek — "they shall by no means find it." THEOLOGICAL: The inversion of the natural desire for life into a longing for death portrays a torment so severe it undoes the most basic human instinct. This echoes Job 3:21 and Jeremiah 8:3, where death becomes preferable to suffering.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the likenesses of the locusts were like horses having been prepared for battle, and upon their heads were as crowns like gold, and their faces were as faces of men.
And they had hair as the hair of women, and their teeth were as those of lions.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: And they had breastplates as breastplates of iron, and the sound of their wings was as the sound of chariots of many horses running into battle.
And they have tails like scorpions, and stings, and in their tails is their authority to harm men for five months.
They have over them a king, the angel of the abyss; his name in Hebrew is Abaddon, and in the Greek he has the name Apollyon.
verses.note: GREEK: Ἀβαδδών (Abaddōn) transliterates the Hebrew אֲבַדּוֹן meaning "destruction" or "place of ruin" (cf. Job 26:6; 28:22; Ps 88:11; Prov 15:11). Ἀπολλύων (Apollyōn, Strong's G623) is the Greek active participle meaning "the Destroyer." DIVINE_COUNCIL: This angel-king of the abyss is a named spiritual ruler exercising dominion over the imprisoned demonic horde. His dual naming in Hebrew and Greek underscores the universal scope of the revelation. HISTORICAL: Some scholars note a possible wordplay with Apollo (Ἀπόλλων), the Greek deity associated with plague — a polemical identification connecting pagan worship to demonic powers.

verses.flow: The first woe has passed; behold, two woes yet come after these things.
And the sixth angel sounded, and I heard one voice out of the horns of the golden altar which is before God,

verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: saying to the sixth angel who had the trumpet, "Release the four angels who have been bound at the great river Euphrates."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Euphrates River was the eastern boundary of the Roman Empire and of the promised land (Gen 15:18; Deut 1:7). It represented the frontier beyond which hostile powers — Parthians, Assyrians, Babylonians — perpetually threatened. DIVINE_COUNCIL: These four angels are "bound" (δεδεμένους, dedeménous, perfect passive participle of déō), indicating they are hostile spiritual beings restrained by divine decree until the appointed moment of release. Their binding parallels the imprisonment motif in 2 Peter 2:4 and Jude 6.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the four angels were released, who had been prepared for the hour and day and month and year, so that they should kill the third of mankind.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The precision of "the hour and day and month and year" (τὴν ὥραν καὶ ἡμέραν καὶ μῆνα καὶ ἐνιαυτόν) — all governed by a single definite article in Greek — emphasizes that this devastating judgment unfolds at one precisely foreordained moment. God's sovereign timetable governs even the release of destructive spiritual powers. GREEK: The singular article τὴν governing all four time nouns treats them as a unified appointed occasion.

verses.flow: And the number of the armies of the horsemen was twice ten thousand times ten thousand; I heard the number of them.
verses.note: GREEK: δισμυριάδες μυριάδων (dismyriádes myriádōn) literally means "two ten-thousands of ten-thousands," yielding 200,000,000. This may be a symbolic number signifying an innumerably vast army rather than a precise count (cf. Ps 68:17; Dan 7:10). STRUCTURE: The shift from the demonic locusts of the fifth trumpet to this immense cavalry marks an escalation — from torment without death to mass killing of one-third of humanity.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And thus I saw the horses in the vision, and those sitting upon them, having breastplates of fire and of hyacinth and of brimstone; and the heads of the horses were as heads of lions, and out of their mouths proceeds fire and smoke and brimstone.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive singular neuter

verses.flow: By these three plagues the third of mankind was killed — by the fire and the smoke and the brimstone proceeding out of their mouths.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: For the authority of the horses is in their mouths and in their tails; for their tails are like serpents, having heads, and with them they do harm.

And the rest of mankind, who were not killed by these plagues, did not even repent of the works of their hands, that they should not worship the demons and the idols of gold and of silver and of bronze and of stone and of wood, which are able neither to see nor to hear nor to walk.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The catalog of idols — gold, silver, bronze, stone, and wood — echoes Daniel 5:4, 5:23 and Psalm 115:4-7; 135:15-17, where idols that cannot see, hear, or walk are contrasted with the living God. GREEK: τὰ δαιμόνια (tá daimónia, Strong's G1140) reveals that behind idol worship stand actual demonic entities — a conviction shared by Paul (1 Cor 10:20) and rooted in the LXX of Deut 32:17 and Ps 106:37. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The refusal to repent despite catastrophic judgment reveals humanity's deep bondage to the demonic realm and the corrupted worship structures of the fallen divine council members.

verses.flow: And they did not repent of their murders, nor of their sorceries, nor of their fornication, nor of their thefts.
verses.note: GREEK: φαρμάκων (pharmákōn, Strong's G5331) denotes sorceries, magic potions, or drug-related enchantments — the root of English "pharmacy." In the ancient world, φαρμακεία was closely linked to both occult practice and poisoning. THEOLOGICAL: The four sins listed — murders, sorceries, fornication, thefts — correspond to violations of the second table of the Decalogue and represent the total moral collapse of a civilization given over to demonic allegiance. The emphatic repetition of "they did not repent" (οὐ μετενόησαν) from v.20 underscores the judicial hardening of those who persist in rebellion against God.

verb perfect middle participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And I saw another mighty angel descending out of heaven, having been clothed with a cloud, and the rainbow upon his head, and his face as the sun, and his feet as pillars of fire,
verses.note: GREEK: "Another mighty angel" (ἄλλον ἄγγελον ἰσχυρόν, allon angelon ischyron) — ἰσχυρός (Strong's G2478) denotes raw power and strength. The description — cloud-wrapped, rainbow-crowned, sun-faced, fire-pillared feet — echoes theophanic imagery from Ezek 1:26-28 and Dan 10:5-6. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Some interpreters identify this as a high-ranking angelic being of the divine council bearing attributes of God's own glory as His commissioned representative; others see a Christophany. The rainbow (ἶρις, G2463) recalls the covenant sign of Gen 9:13 and the throne-room vision of Rev 4:3.

verses.flow: and having in his hand a little scroll having been opened. And he set his right foot upon the sea, and the left upon the earth,
and he cried out with a great voice, even as a lion roars. And when he cried out, the seven thunders spoke their own voices.
verses.note: GREEK: μυκᾶται (mykatai, Strong's G3455) means "to roar" or "to bellow," used only here in the NT (hapax legomenon), typically of cattle but here applied to a lion's roar. TYPOLOGY: The lion's roar echoes Amos 3:8 and Hos 11:10, where God roars as a lion before revealing His purposes. The seven thunders (ἑπτὰ βρονταί) may allude to the sevenfold voice of the Lord in Ps 29, expressing divine authority over creation.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: And when the seven thunders spoke, I was about to write; and I heard a voice out of heaven saying, "Seal up the things which the seven thunders spoke, and do not write them."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The sealing of the seven thunders' words is unique in Revelation — a deliberate withholding of divine revelation. This stands in contrast to Rev 22:10, where John is told not to seal the words of this prophecy. σφράγισον (sphragison, Strong's G4972) is an aorist imperative — a decisive, immediate command. God retains sovereign mysteries even within the context of apocalyptic disclosure (Deut 29:29).

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the angel whom I saw standing upon the sea and upon the earth lifted up his right hand to heaven,

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: and swore by the One living unto the ages of the ages, who created the heaven and the things in it, and the earth and the things in it, and the sea and the things in it, that time shall be no longer,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The angel's oath echoes Dan 12:7, where a heavenly figure swears by the Eternal One. The oath formula — "the One living unto the ages of the ages, who created heaven, earth, and sea and all things in them" — affirms God as eternal Creator (cf. Exod 20:11; Neh 9:6; Acts 14:15). "Time shall be no longer" (χρόνος οὐκέτι ἔσται) signals not annihilation of time but that delay is ended; God's patience gives way to consummation.

verses.flow: but in the days of the voice of the seventh angel, whenever he is about to sound the trumpet, then the mystery of God is accomplished, as He proclaimed the good tidings to His own servants the prophets.
verses.note: GREEK: τὸ μυστήριον τοῦ θεοῦ (to mystērion tou theou) — μυστήριον (Strong's G3466) denotes a divine secret now disclosed, not esoteric hidden knowledge. TYPOLOGY: The "mystery of God" accomplished at the seventh trumpet fulfills the prophetic expectation announced through the prophets (Amos 3:7). εὐηγγέλισεν (euēngelisen, "proclaimed good tidings") uses the same root as "gospel," indicating that even final judgment constitutes the fulfillment of God's redemptive good news.

verses.flow: And the voice which I heard out of heaven was again speaking with me and saying, "Go, take the scroll which has been opened in the hand of the angel who stands upon the sea and upon the earth."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And I went to the angel, telling him to give me the little scroll. And he says to me, "Take and devour it, and it shall make bitter your stomach, but in your mouth it shall be sweet as honey."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The command to eat the scroll directly parallels Ezek 2:8-3:3, where Ezekiel is told to eat a scroll that is sweet as honey in his mouth but contains words of lamentation and woe. GREEK: βιβλαρίδιον (biblidaridion, Strong's G974) is a diminutive of βιβλίον, "little scroll," used only in Revelation. κατάφαγε (kataphage, Strong's G2719) means "devour completely" — not merely taste but wholly internalize. The bitterness in the stomach signifies the painful content of prophetic judgment that must be proclaimed.

verses.flow: And I took the little scroll out of the hand of the angel and devoured it, and it was in my mouth sweet as honey; and when I ate it, my stomach was made bitter.
And they say to me, "It is necessary for you to prophesy again concerning peoples and nations and tongues and many kings."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The commission to "prophesy again" (πάλιν προφητεῦσαι) indicates that John's prophetic task is not yet complete. The fourfold designation — peoples, nations, tongues, and kings — is a universal formula in Revelation (cf. Rev 5:9; 7:9; 11:9; 14:6), signifying the global scope of the prophetic message. GREEK: δεῖ (dei, Strong's G1163) expresses divine necessity — "it is binding upon you" — underscoring that this is not optional but a mandate from the divine council.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And there was given to me a reed like a rod, saying, "Rise and measure the temple of God and the altar and those worshiping in it.
verses.note: GREEK: "Kalamos" (κάλαμος, Strong's G2563) denotes a measuring reed or rod, a hollow cane used as a standard of measurement. "Naos" (ναός, G3485) refers specifically to the inner sanctuary or shrine, not the broader temple complex (hieron). The command to measure signifies divine protection and ownership of what is measured. TYPOLOGY: The measuring of the temple echoes Ezekiel 40-42, where an angelic figure measures the eschatological temple, signifying God's sovereign claim over His dwelling and His people.

verses.flow: And the court that is outside the temple, cast it out and do not measure it, for it has been given to the nations, and the holy city they will trample for forty-two months.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The outer court given to the nations and the trampling of the holy city for forty-two months parallels Daniel 7:25 and 12:7, establishing a period of Gentile dominion over the holy place. HISTORICAL: "Trampling" (πατέω, G3961) the holy city recalls Luke 21:24 where Jesus prophesied that Jerusalem would be trampled by the nations until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled. The forty-two months (3.5 years) is a recurring prophetic time period tied to Daniel's framework.

words.m: verb perfect middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And I will give to my two witnesses, and they will prophesy one thousand two hundred and sixty days, having been clothed in sackcloth.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: These are the two olive trees and the two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The two olive trees directly reference Zechariah 4:3, 11-14, where they represent "the two sons of oil" standing before the Lord of all the earth—Joshua the high priest and Zerubbabel the governor. The lampstands recall Revelation 1:20 where lampstands represent churches. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Their standing "before the Lord of the earth" (ἐνώπιον τοῦ κυρίου τῆς γῆς) positions these witnesses as agents of divine authority operating in the heavenly council's commission upon earth.

verses.flow: And if anyone desires to harm them, fire proceeds out of their mouth and devours their enemies; and if anyone should desire to harm them, thus it is necessary for him to be killed.

These have the authority to shut the heaven, so that rain may not fall during the days of their prophecy, and they have authority over the waters to turn them into blood and to strike the earth with every plague, as often as they may desire.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And when they finish their testimony, the beast that ascends out of the abyss will make war with them and will overcome them and will kill them.
verses.note: GREEK: "Thērion" (θηρίον, G2342) denotes a wild beast and is distinct from "zōon" (living creature) used of the heavenly beings in chapter 4. The beast ascending "ek tēs abyssou" (ἐκ τῆς ἀβύσσου)—out of the abyss—marks its origin in the realm of imprisoned spiritual powers (cf. Rev 9:1-2, 11; Luke 8:31). THEOLOGICAL: This is the first appearance of "the beast" who will dominate Revelation 13 and 17, here introduced as the agent of satanic opposition who can act only when God's witnesses have completed (τελέσωσιν) their appointed testimony.

verses.flow: And their dead body will lie upon the street of the great city, which is called spiritually Sodom and Egypt, where also their Lord was crucified.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The great city is called "spiritually" (πνευματικῶς, G4153—a rare adverb occurring only here in Revelation) Sodom and Egypt, combining images of moral depravity (Gen 19) and oppressive enslavement (Exodus). The identification "where also their Lord was crucified" points to Jerusalem, yet the symbolic naming universalizes the city as representative of all worldly rebellion against God. GREEK: Pneumatikōs is a significant adverb indicating that the names Sodom and Egypt function as spiritual-typological designations, not merely geographical ones.

verses.flow: And those from the peoples and tribes and tongues and nations look upon their dead body for three days and a half, and they do not permit their dead bodies to be placed into a tomb.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And those dwelling upon the earth rejoice over them and make merry, and they will send gifts to one another, because these two prophets tormented those dwelling upon the earth.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And after the three days and a half, a spirit of life from God entered into them, and they stood upon their feet, and great fear fell upon those beholding them.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The resurrection of the two witnesses after three and a half days echoes the resurrection of Christ and the vision of Ezekiel 37:5, 10 where the Spirit of God (πνεῦμα ζωῆς, "spirit of life") enters the dry bones and they stand upon their feet. THEOLOGICAL: The pattern of faithful testimony, death at the hands of evil powers, and divine vindication through resurrection recapitulates the Christ-pattern and demonstrates God's sovereign reversal of apparent defeat.

verses.flow: And they heard a great voice from heaven saying to them, "Come up here!" And they ascended into heaven in the cloud, and their enemies beheld them.

And in that hour there came a great earthquake, and a tenth of the city fell, and there were killed in the earthquake seven thousand names of men, and the rest became terrified and gave glory to the God of heaven.
The second woe has passed; behold, the third woe comes quickly.

verses.flow: And the seventh angel sounded, and there came great voices in heaven, saying, "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ, and He shall reign unto the ages of the ages."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The seventh trumpet constitutes the climactic announcement of God's eschatological reign. "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ" (ἐγένετο ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ κόσμου τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν καὶ τοῦ Χριστοῦ αὐτοῦ) fulfills the promise of Psalm 2:2, 6-8 and Daniel 2:44; 7:14, 27. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The singular "He shall reign" (βασιλεύσει) affirms the unity of the Father and His Anointed One in sovereign kingship, echoing the divine council decree of Psalm 2 where the Lord and His Messiah act as one.

verses.note: GREEK: The divine title "the One who is and who was" (ὁ ὢν καὶ ὁ ἦν) notably omits the expected third element "and who is to come" (ὁ ἐρχόμενος), found in Rev 1:4, 8 and 4:8. The omission is theologically deliberate: the coming has now been realized—God has arrived in His reign. THEOLOGICAL: "You have taken Your great power and have begun to reign" (ἐβασίλευσας, ingressive aorist) marks the decisive moment when God's latent sovereignty becomes manifest, active, and unopposed in history.

verses.flow: And the nations were enraged, and Your wrath has come, and the time of the dead to be judged, and to give the reward to Your servants the prophets and to the saints and to those fearing Your name, the small and the great, and to destroy those who destroy the earth."
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This verse provides a comprehensive summary of eschatological events: (1) the raging of the nations (Ps 2:1), (2) the coming of God's wrath, (3) the judgment of the dead, (4) the rewarding of the faithful, and (5) the destruction of the destroyers. TYPOLOGY: "The nations were enraged" (ὠργίσθησαν τὰ ἔθνη) directly quotes LXX Psalm 2:1, framing all earthly opposition to God's reign within the Messianic psalm's prophetic framework. The fourfold designation of the rewardable—servants, prophets, saints, and those fearing God's name—encompasses the entire people of God.

verses.flow: And the temple of God was opened, the one in heaven, and the ark of His covenant was seen in His temple, and there came lightnings and voices and thunders and an earthquake and great hail.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The appearance of the ark of the covenant (κιβωτὸς τῆς διαθήκης, G2787/G1242) in the heavenly temple signifies the consummation of God's covenant faithfulness. The earthly ark was lost after the Babylonian destruction of the temple (cf. Jer 3:16; 2 Macc 2:4-8); its heavenly manifestation reveals that God's covenant purposes were never lost but preserved in the true sanctuary. THEOLOGICAL: The theophanic phenomena—lightnings, voices, thunders, earthquake, and hail—echo Sinai (Exod 19:16-18) and the throne room vision of Rev 4:5, indicating that the covenant-making God now acts in final judgment and redemption.

verses.flow: And a great sign appeared in heaven: a woman clothed with the sun, and the moon beneath her feet, and upon her head a crown of twelve stars.
verses.note: GREEK: σημεῖον (sēmeion, G4592) means "sign" in the sense of a significant portent, not merely a miracle. περιβεβλημένη (peribeblēmenē) is a perfect passive participle of περιβάλλω, "having been clothed around." TYPOLOGY: The woman's imagery—sun, moon, twelve stars—echoes Joseph's dream in Gen 37:9, identifying her with Israel. The twelve stars correspond to the twelve tribes, placing this vision in the context of God's covenant people from whom the Messiah comes.

verses.flow: And his tail drags a third of the stars of heaven, and he cast them to the earth. And the dragon stood before the woman who was about to give birth, so that when she should bring forth her child, he might devour it.
And she bore a son, a male, who is about to shepherd all the nations with a rod of iron; and her child was caught up to God and to his throne.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The male child who will "shepherd all the nations with a rod of iron" is a direct citation of Ps 2:9 (LXX), a royal messianic psalm. GREEK: ἡρπάσθη (hērpasthē) from ἁρπάζω (G726) means "was snatched up" suddenly, the same verb used for the rapture in 1 Thess 4:17, here referring to Christ's ascension and enthronement.

This heavenly war reflects divine council conflict where rebellious members of God's heavenly host are expelled. The war is not between equals; Michael acts under God's authority to execute the dragon's expulsion following Christ's victorious work.

verses.flow: And the great dragon was cast out—the ancient serpent, the one called the Devil and Satan, the one deceiving the whole inhabited world—he was cast to the earth, and his angels were cast out with him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Four titles identify the dragon: (1) the great dragon, (2) the ancient serpent (linking to Gen 3:1-15), (3) the Devil (διάβολος, G1228, "slanderer"), (4) Satan (Σατανᾶς, G4567, Hebrew "adversary"). GREEK: πλανῶν (planōn, G4105) is a present active participle—"the one continually deceiving"—emphasizing ongoing deceptive activity over the whole οἰκουμένη (oikoumenē), the inhabited world. DIVINE_COUNCIL: His casting to earth fulfills the pattern of Isa 14:12 and Ezek 28:16-17, the expulsion of a rebellious divine being from God's presence.

verses.flow: And I heard a great voice in heaven, saying, "Now has come the salvation and the power and the kingdom of our God, and the authority of his Christ, for the accuser of our brothers has been cast out, the one accusing them before our God day and night."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The heavenly voice declares four realities now actualized: salvation (σωτηρία), power (δύναμις), kingdom (βασιλεία) of God, and the authority (ἐξουσία) of his Christ. GREEK: κατήγωρ (katēgōr, G2725) is a variant form of κατήγορος ("accuser"), a legal term. This is the heavenly courtroom scene paralleling Job 1-2 and Zech 3:1, where Satan's role as prosecuting accuser before God's throne is now terminated by Christ's victory.

verses.flow: "And they conquered him through the blood of the Lamb, and through the word of their testimony, and they did not love their life even unto death."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Three means of victory are named: the blood of the Lamb (substitutionary atonement), the word of their testimony (faithful witness), and willingness to die (martyrdom). GREEK: ἐνίκησαν (enikēsan, G3528) is the aorist of νικάω, "they conquered decisively." The paradox is central: believers conquer precisely through apparent defeat, mirroring the Lamb who conquers through being slain (Rev 5:5-6).

verses.flow: And the dragon was enraged at the woman, and went away to make war with the rest of her offspring—those keeping the commandments of God and holding the testimony of Jesus.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "rest of her offspring" (τῶν λοιπῶν τοῦ σπέρματος αὐτῆς) are identified by two marks: keeping God's commandments and holding the testimony of Jesus. TYPOLOGY: This is the protoevangelium fulfilled—Gen 3:15's "seed of the woman" at enmity with the serpent. The woman's seed is both the Christ (v.5) and those united to him by faith, against whom the dragon's warfare now turns. GREEK: μαρτυρίαν Ἰησοῦ (martyrian Iēsou) can be objective genitive ("testimony about Jesus") or subjective ("testimony that Jesus bore"); both senses are likely intended.

verses.flow: And I saw out of the sea a beast rising up, having ten horns and seven heads, and upon its horns ten diadems, and upon its heads names of blasphemy.
verses.note: GREEK: θηρίον (thērion, G2342) denotes a wild, venomous beast — distinct from ζῷον (zōon, "living creature") used of the heavenly beings in Rev 4. The term emphasizes savagery and predatory nature. TYPOLOGY: The beast from the sea with seven heads and ten horns directly parallels the fourth beast of Daniel 7:3-7 and the composite creatures of Daniel's vision, representing successive world empires culminating in a final anti-God dominion. The sea in prophetic symbolism represents the chaotic nations (Isa 17:12-13; Dan 7:2-3). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The ten diadems (διαδήματα, royal crowns, not στέφανοι/victory wreaths) represent usurped kingly authority — a counterfeit of Christ's many diadems (Rev 19:12).

verses.flow: And the beast which I saw was like a leopard, and its feet as of a bear, and its mouth as the mouth of a lion. And the dragon gave to it his power and his throne and great authority.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The composite beast combines features of all four beasts in Daniel 7 — the leopard (Greece/Dan 7:6), the bear (Medo-Persia/Dan 7:5), and the lion (Babylon/Dan 7:4) — but in reverse order, as John looks backward in time. This beast embodies the totality of rebellious human empire. THEOLOGICAL: The dragon giving his power (δύναμιν), throne (θρόνον), and authority (ἐξουσίαν) is a dark parody of the Father granting all authority to the Son (Matt 28:18; John 5:22). This constitutes a satanic anti-trinity.

verses.flow: And one of its heads was as having been slain unto death, and the wound of its death was healed. And the whole earth marveled after the beast.
verses.note: GREEK: ἐσφαγμένην (esphagmenēn, G4969) — "having been slain" — is the same participle used of the Lamb in Rev 5:6. This is a deliberate counterfeit of Christ's death and resurrection. THEOLOGICAL: The healed death-wound produces false wonder (ἐθαυμάσθη) that leads to false worship, parodying how Christ's resurrection leads to true worship. The beast mimics the paschal pattern of death-and-life that belongs to Christ alone.

verses.flow: And they worshiped the dragon, because he gave the authority to the beast, and they worshiped the beast, saying, "Who is like the beast, and who is able to make war with it?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Who is like the beast?" (Τίς ὅμοιος τῷ θηρίῳ) is a blasphemous inversion of the divine name Michael (מִיכָאֵל, "Who is like God?" — Dan 12:1) and of the praise of Exodus 15:11: "Who is like you among the gods, O Lord?" The worship of the dragon through the beast reveals the ultimate satanic aim: to receive the worship due to God alone. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This passage exposes the spiritual warfare behind imperial cult worship — the principalities and powers (Eph 6:12) receiving illicit worship through human political structures.

verses.flow: And there was given to it a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies, and there was given to it authority to act for forty-two months.
verses.note: GREEK: ἐδόθη (edothē) — the divine passive "it was given" — appears repeatedly in this chapter (vv. 5, 7, 14, 15), indicating that even the beast's authority operates under God's sovereign permission. HISTORICAL: The forty-two months (τεσσεράκοντα δύο μῆνας) equals 1,260 days or three and a half years, echoing Daniel 7:25 and 12:7, the period of tribulation under the "little horn." This timeframe recurs in Rev 11:2-3 and 12:6, 14.

verses.flow: And all who dwell upon the earth shall worship it, everyone whose name has not been written in the book of life of the Lamb who has been slain from the foundation of the world.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "book of life of the Lamb who has been slain from the foundation of the world" (ἀπὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου) is one of the most profound predestination texts in Scripture, linking the Lamb's sacrificial death to God's eternal purpose before creation (1 Pet 1:19-20; Eph 1:4). GREEK: καταβολή (katabolē, G2602) literally means "a casting down" or "laying of a foundation." The phrase may modify either "written" or "slain" — both yield profound theology: either names were written before creation, or the Lamb was foreordained to be slain before creation.

verses.flow: If anyone has an ear, let him hear.
If anyone is for captivity, into captivity he goes. If anyone is to be killed by the sword, by the sword he must be killed. Here is the endurance and the faith of the saints.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This verse serves as a prophetic interlude of pastoral exhortation within the apocalyptic vision, recalling the pattern of Jeremiah 15:2 and 43:11. THEOLOGICAL: ὑπομονή (hypomonē, G5281) denotes not passive resignation but active, persevering endurance under trial. Combined with πίστις (pistis, "faith/faithfulness"), it calls the saints to resist the beast through faithful suffering rather than armed rebellion — the way of the Lamb, not the way of the sword (Matt 26:52).

verses.flow: And I saw another beast rising up out of the earth, and it had two horns like a lamb, and it spoke as a dragon.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The second beast rises from the earth (γῆ) rather than the sea, and is later called the "false prophet" (Rev 16:13; 19:20; 20:10). Its two horns like a lamb (ἀρνίῳ) yet dragon-speech perfectly embody Christ's warning about false prophets who come in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves (Matt 7:15). DIVINE_COUNCIL: This figure completes the unholy trinity — the dragon (counterfeit Father), the sea beast (counterfeit Son), and the earth beast/false prophet (counterfeit Spirit), who directs worship toward the first beast as the Spirit directs worship toward Christ (John 16:14).

verses.flow: And it deceives those who dwell upon the earth through the signs which were given to it to perform before the beast, telling those who dwell upon the earth to make an image to the beast who has the wound of the sword and lived.
And it was given to it to give breath to the image of the beast, so that the image of the beast should both speak and cause that as many as would not worship the image of the beast should be killed.
verses.note: GREEK: πνεῦμα (pneuma, G4151) here means "breath" or "spirit" — the false prophet gives animating breath to the image, counterfeiting God's act of breathing life into Adam (Gen 2:7; LXX πνοὴν ζωῆς). HISTORICAL: Greco-Roman temples employed mechanical devices, ventriloquism, and priestly deception to make cult statues appear to speak — a practice well-documented by Lucian and other ancient sources. The text may envision something beyond mere trickery, however, involving genuine demonic empowerment.

verses.flow: And it causes all, the small and the great, and the rich and the poor, and the free and the slaves, that they should give them a mark upon their right hand or upon their forehead,

verses.flow: Here is wisdom. Let the one having understanding count the number of the beast, for it is the number of a man, and his number is six hundred sixty-six.
verses.note: GREEK: ψηφισάτω (psēphisatō, G5585) means "let him calculate/count with pebbles" — from ψῆφος (psēphos), a voting or counting stone. The number 666 (ἑξακόσιοι ἑξήκοντα ἕξ) is identified as ἀριθμὸς ἀνθρώπου — "a number of a man" or "a human number." HISTORICAL: In both Hebrew and Greek, letters served as numerals (gematria/isopsephy). The most widely attested ancient identification is Nero Caesar (נרון קסר = 50+200+6+50+100+60+200 = 666). Some manuscripts read 616, which corresponds to the Latin form "Nero Cesar" without the final nun. THEOLOGICAL: Whether pointing to a specific historical figure or a future one, the number 666 falls perpetually short of the divine number seven — a trinity of incompleteness, humanity grasping at divinity and forever failing.

verses.flow: And another angel came out from the altar, the one having authority over the fire, and he called with a great voice to the one having the sharp sickle, saying, "Send forth your sharp sickle and gather the clusters of the vine of the earth, for her grapes are fully ripe."
And the angel cast his sickle into the earth, and gathered the vine of the earth, and cast it into the great winepress of the fury of God.
And the winepress was trodden outside the city, and blood came out from the winepress, up to the bridles of the horses, for one thousand six hundred stadia.
verses.note: GREEK: "Stadia chiliōn hexakosiōn" (σταδίων χιλίων ἑξακοσίων) — 1,600 stadia — is approximately 184 miles. Some see the number symbolically as 4 × 4 × 100, representing universal completeness of judgment across the four corners of the earth. TYPOLOGY: The winepress of God's wrath echoes Isa 63:1-6, where the Lord treads the winepress alone in garments stained with blood. The blood reaching the horses' bridles is hyperbolic imagery of catastrophic, total judgment (cf. Joel 3:13).

verses.flow: And I saw another sign in heaven, great and marvelous: seven angels having seven plagues, the last ones, for in them the wrath of God was completed.
verses.note: GREEK: "Sēmeion" (σημεῖον, Strong's G4592) means "sign" — a portent pointing beyond itself to divine reality. "Etelesthē" (ἐτελέσθη) from "teleō" (G5055) means "was completed/finished," the same root as Christ's cry "tetelestai" in John 19:30. The seven last plagues bring God's wrath (thymos, G2372) to its consummation. STRUCTURE: This chapter serves as a heavenly prelude to the seven bowl judgments of chapter 16, paralleling the heavenly throne-room scenes before the seals (ch. 4-5) and trumpets (ch. 8:1-5).

verses.flow: And I saw as it were a sea of glass mingled with fire, and those who conquer out of the beast and out of its image and out of the number of its name, standing upon the sea of glass, having harps of God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And they sing the song of Moses, the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb, saying: Great and marvelous are your works, Lord God, the Almighty; righteous and true are your ways, O King of the ages.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "song of Moses" recalls Exodus 15, sung after Israel's deliverance through the Red Sea — now the redeemed sing it together with the "song of the Lamb," uniting the Exodus deliverance typology with its ultimate fulfillment in Christ's redemption (Exod 15:1-18; Deut 32:1-43). The title "King of the ages" (basileus tōn aiōnōn) echoes the eternal kingship ascribed to YHWH in the Hebrew Scriptures (Jer 10:10; Ps 145:13). GREEK: "Pantokratōr" (παντοκράτωρ, G3841) renders Hebrew "Shaddai" or "YHWH Sabaoth" in the LXX — "the All-Sovereign," emphasizing God's absolute dominion over all powers, including hostile spiritual beings.

verses.flow: Who shall not fear, O Lord, and glorify your name? For you alone are holy; for all the nations shall come and worship before you, for your righteous judgments have been made manifest.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse echoes Psalm 86:9 (LXX 85:9) and Jeremiah 10:7, affirming the eschatological hope that all nations will worship before God. "Hosios" (ὅσιος, G3741) means "holy" in the sense of pious, devout, or covenantally faithful — distinct from "hagios" (set apart). It is applied to God as the one whose character is inherently faithful and pure. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The declaration "you alone are holy" sets the true God apart from all other claimants to divine status, echoing the incomparability formula of Deuteronomy 32:39 and the divine council challenge of Psalm 89:6-8.

verses.flow: And after these things I looked, and the temple of the tent of the testimony in heaven was opened,
verses.note: GREEK: "Naos tēs skēnēs tou martyriou" (ναὸς τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου) — "the temple of the tent of the testimony" — combines tabernacle and temple imagery. "Naos" (G3485) refers to the inner sanctuary, not the broader temple complex (hieron). "Martyrion" (G3142) recalls the "tent of witness/testimony" (Num 17:7-8 LXX), housing the ark and the covenant tablets. TYPOLOGY: The heavenly temple opening echoes Revelation 11:19 and signifies that the judgments originate from God's very presence and covenant faithfulness — the earthly tabernacle was always a copy of this heavenly reality (Heb 8:5; Exod 25:9).

verses.flow: And the seven angels having the seven plagues came forth out of the temple, clothed in clean bright linen, and girded about the chest with golden sashes.

verses.flow: And one of the four living creatures gave to the seven angels seven golden bowls full of the wrath of God, the one living unto the ages of the ages.
And the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God and from his power, and no one was able to enter into the temple until the seven plagues of the seven angels were completed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The temple filled with smoke from God's glory recalls the dedication of Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11) and Isaiah's throne-room vision (Isa 6:4), where the kabod (glory-presence) of God was so overwhelming that no ministry could continue. That no one could enter until the plagues were completed signifies the irrevocable nature of the coming judgments — there is no intercession that can halt them. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The glory and power (doxa kai dynamis) filling the heavenly sanctuary demonstrate the unapproachable majesty of God in his role as cosmic judge, executing decrees from the divine council throne room.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: And I heard a great voice out of the temple, saying to the seven angels, "Go and pour out the seven bowls of the wrath of God upon the earth."
verses.note: GREEK: "Phialē" (φιάλη, Strong's G5357) denotes a broad, shallow bowl used in temple libations, not a narrow vial. The word "thymos" (θυμός, G2372) signifies fierce, burning wrath — distinct from "orgē" (settled indignation), though both appear together in v19. The voice issues from the "naos" (inner sanctuary), indicating the command originates from God's very presence. TYPOLOGY: The seven bowls parallel the ten plagues of Egypt (Exod 7-12), now poured out in eschatological fullness upon the whole earth rather than upon one nation.

verses.flow: And the first went and poured out his bowl upon the earth, and there came a grievous and evil sore upon the men having the mark of the beast and those worshiping its image.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The grievous sores (ἕλκος, hélkos, G1668) directly echo the sixth plague of Egypt — boils upon the Egyptians (Exod 9:8-12, LXX also uses hélkos). The plague falls specifically on beast-worshipers, marking a distinction between covenant people and idolaters as in the Exodus narrative.

verses.flow: And the second poured out his bowl into the sea, and it became blood as of a dead man, and every living soul died — the things in the sea.
And the third poured out his bowl into the rivers and the springs of the waters, and they became blood.
