verses.flow: And I heard the angel of the waters saying, "Righteous are you, the One who is and who was, the Holy One, because you judged these things;
verses.note: GREEK: "Ho ōn kai ho ēn, ho hosios" — "the One who is and who was, the Holy One." The title "ho hosios" (ὅσιος, G3741) denotes covenant faithfulness and sacred purity, distinct from "hagios." Some manuscripts read "ho esomenos" (who is to come), but its absence here suggests the coming has now arrived in judgment. THEOLOGICAL: The angel of the waters declares a lex talionis theology — God's judgments correspond precisely to the crimes committed.

verses.flow: for they poured out the blood of saints and prophets, and blood you have given them to drink — worthy they are."

verses.flow: And I heard the altar saying, "Yes, Lord God, the Almighty, true and righteous are your judgments."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The altar itself speaks — a striking personification recalling the martyrs' cry from beneath the altar (Rev 6:9-10). Their prayer for vindication now receives its answer. The doxology "true and righteous are your judgments" echoes Ps 19:9 and Deut 32:4, affirming divine justice. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The altar as a speaking witness places the heavenly furniture itself among the cosmic voices affirming God's judicial decree.

verses.flow: And the fourth poured out his bowl upon the sun, and it was given to it to scorch men with fire.

verses.flow: And men were scorched with great heat, and they blasphemed the name of God, the One having authority over these plagues, and they did not repent to give him glory.

verses.flow: And the fifth poured out his bowl upon the throne of the beast, and its kingdom became darkened, and they were gnawing their tongues from the pain,
and they blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pains and because of their sores, and they did not repent of their works.
And the sixth poured out his bowl upon the great river, the Euphrates, and its water was dried up, so that the way of the kings from the rising of the sun might be prepared.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Euphrates was the eastern boundary of the Roman Empire and of the promised land (Gen 15:18; Deut 1:7). Parthian armies from the east were Rome's most feared enemy. TYPOLOGY: The drying of the Euphrates echoes the drying of the Red Sea (Exod 14:21) and the Jordan (Josh 3:17), but here inverted — the way is prepared not for God's people but for hostile kings marshaled for destruction.

verses.flow: And I saw out of the mouth of the dragon, and out of the mouth of the beast, and out of the mouth of the false prophet, three unclean spirits like frogs;
verses.note: GREEK: "Pseudoprophētēs" (ψευδοπροφήτης, G5578) appears here for the first time in Revelation, identifying the second beast of chapter 13 with the role of false prophet. The unclean spirits "like frogs" (hōs bátrachoi) echo the second Egyptian plague (Exod 8:1-15). DIVINE_COUNCIL: This is an anti-trinity — the dragon (parody of the Father), the beast (parody of Christ), and the false prophet (parody of the Holy Spirit) — each emitting a deceiving spirit from its mouth.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: for they are spirits of demons, performing signs, which go out upon the kings of the whole inhabited world, to gather them unto the war of the great day of God the Almighty —
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The spirits of demons perform "sēmeia" (σημεῖα, signs) — the same word used for Jesus's miracles in John's Gospel, showing that miraculous signs alone do not validate divine origin (cf. Deut 13:1-3; Matt 24:24). The gathering is "eis ton polemon" — unto THE war, with the article indicating the definitive, eschatological battle. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Even demonic activity is subordinated to God's sovereign purpose; they gather kings for "the great day of God the Almighty," fulfilling his decree.

verses.flow: "Behold, I come as a thief. Blessed is the one watching and keeping his garments, lest he walk naked and they see his shame" —
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This parenthetical saying of Christ interrupts the sixth bowl narrative, echoing his words in Matt 24:43-44 and Rev 3:3. The blessing formula ("makarios") is the third of seven beatitudes in Revelation (cf. 1:3; 14:13; 16:15; 19:9; 20:6; 22:7; 22:14). TYPOLOGY: The imagery of keeping garments recalls the high priest's vigilance in the temple and the Levitical requirement of purity; nakedness signifies covenant shame (Gen 3:7; Ezek 16:37).

verses.flow: and they gathered them into the place called in Hebrew, Armageddon.
verses.note: GREEK: "Armagedōn" (Ἁρμαγεδών) is a hapax legomenon in the entire Bible. It is generally understood as a transliteration of Hebrew "Har Megiddo" (הר מגדו), "Mountain of Megiddo." HISTORICAL: Megiddo was the site of decisive battles in Israel's history — Deborah and Barak (Judg 5:19), Josiah's death (2 Kgs 23:29), and Thutmose III's famous battle. Paradoxically, Megiddo sits in a valley, not on a mountain, suggesting a symbolic-typological rather than strictly geographical designation.

verses.flow: And the seventh poured out his bowl upon the air — and there came forth a great voice out of the temple, from the throne, saying, "It is done!"
And there came lightnings and voices and thunders, and there was a great earthquake, such as had not come to be since men came to be upon the earth — so mighty an earthquake, so great.
And the great city came to be into three parts, and the cities of the nations fell. And Babylon the great was remembered before God, to give to her the cup of the wine of the fury of his wrath.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The cup of the wine of the fury of his wrath combines two distinct wrath-words: "thymos" (burning fury) and "orgē" (settled wrath), intensifying the judgment. Babylon's cup echoes Jer 25:15-29, where God commands the prophet to make the nations drink the cup of wrath. STRUCTURE: The splitting of the great city into three parts reverses the triadic unity of the dragon, beast, and false prophet, while anticipating the detailed fall of Babylon in chapters 17-18.

verses.flow: And every island fled, and mountains were not found.
And great hail, about the weight of a talent, comes down out of heaven upon men; and men blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail, for exceedingly great is the plague thereof.
verses.note: GREEK: "Talantiaía" (ταλαντιαία, G5006) — a hapax legomenon meaning "weighing a talent," approximately 75-100 pounds (34-45 kg). This is the most extreme hail imagery in Scripture. TYPOLOGY: The plague of hail completes the Exodus plague typology (Exod 9:22-26), escalated to cosmic proportions. The response of blasphemy rather than repentance mirrors the hardening of Pharaoh's heart, demonstrating that even the most severe judgments do not produce repentance in the rebellious heart.

verses.flow: And one of the seven angels having the seven bowls came and spoke with me, saying, "Come, I will show you the judgment of the great harlot who sits upon many waters,
verses.note: GREEK: "Harlot" (πόρνης, pórnēs; Strong's G4204) denotes a prostitute, used metaphorically in prophetic tradition for idolatrous and unfaithful cities (Isa 1:21; Nah 3:4). "Sits upon many waters" (καθημένης ἐπὶ ὑδάτων πολλῶν) echoes Jer 51:13 LXX, where Babylon is described as dwelling upon many waters — linking this figure typologically to ancient Babylon. TYPOLOGY: The judgment scene parallels the prophetic oracles against Babylon (Isa 13; Jer 50-51) and Tyre (Ezek 26-28), where great commercial and imperial powers face divine reckoning.

verses.flow: And he carried me away into a wilderness in the Spirit. And I saw a woman sitting upon a scarlet beast, full of names of blasphemy, having seven heads and ten horns.
verses.note: GREEK: "In the Spirit" (ἐν πνεύματι) indicates a visionary state of prophetic transport, as in Rev 1:10, 4:2, and 21:10. The "scarlet beast" (θηρίον κόκκινον) recalls the beast of Rev 13:1, now explicitly colored scarlet, linking it to imperial power and bloodshed. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The seven heads and ten horns parallel Daniel 7:7-24, where bestial empires represent rebellious human kingdoms empowered by hostile spiritual powers opposed to the Most High's council.

verses.flow: And the woman was clothed in purple and scarlet, and gilded with gold and precious stone and pearls, having a golden cup in her hand full of abominations and the unclean things of her fornication,

verses.flow: and upon her forehead a name having been written: MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF THE HARLOTS AND OF THE ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH.
verses.note: GREEK: "Mystery" (μυστήριον, mystērion; Strong's G3466) here functions as a label indicating that the name is symbolic and requires spiritual discernment, not a literal title. TYPOLOGY: "Babylon the Great" functions as a typological cipher — as historical Babylon was the archetypal oppressor of God's people and destroyer of the temple (2 Kgs 25), this eschatological Babylon embodies the culmination of all anti-God imperial systems. "Mother of harlots" indicates she is the source and archetype of all idolatrous corruption.

verses.flow: The beast that you saw was, and is not, and is about to ascend out of the abyss and goes to destruction. And those dwelling upon the earth shall marvel — those whose name has not been written upon the book of life from the foundation of the world — when they see the beast, that it was, and is not, and shall be present.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The formula "was, and is not, and is about to ascend" is a demonic parody of the divine title of God who "is and was and is coming" (Rev 1:4, 8; 4:8). The beast's origin from the abyss (ἀβύσσου, abyssou; Strong's G12) marks it as belonging to the realm of imprisoned hostile spiritual powers (Rev 9:1-2, 11; 11:7; 20:1-3). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "book of life from the foundation of the world" (βιβλίον τῆς ζωῆς ἀπὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου) points to divine sovereign election operative before cosmic history, securing the saints against deception (cf. Rev 13:8; Eph 1:4).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Here is the mind having wisdom: the seven heads are seven mountains upon which the woman sits, and they are seven kings.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Seven mountains" (ἑπτὰ ὄρη) was widely recognized in antiquity as a reference to Rome, famously called the city on seven hills (Virgil, Aeneid 6.783; Cicero, Ad Atticum 6.5). The dual identification as both mountains and kings indicates that the symbolism operates on multiple levels — geographic, political, and spiritual. STRUCTURE: The shift from topographical to royal symbolism shows the apocalyptic layering characteristic of Revelation's visions.

verses.flow: The five have fallen, the one is, the other has not yet come, and when he comes he must remain a little while.
And the beast that was and is not, he himself is an eighth and is of the seven, and goes to destruction.
And the ten horns that you saw are ten kings who have not yet received a kingdom, but they receive authority as kings for one hour with the beast.
These have one purpose, and they give their power and authority to the beast.
These shall make war with the Lamb, and the Lamb shall conquer them, for He is Lord of lords and King of kings, and those with Him are called and chosen and faithful."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Lord of lords and King of kings" (κύριος κυρίων... καὶ βασιλεὺς βασιλέων) is the supreme christological title in Revelation, applied to the Lamb and echoed in Rev 19:16. It draws on Deut 10:17 and Dan 2:47, where it describes God Himself — here applied directly to Christ, affirming His full divine sovereignty. TYPOLOGY: The Lamb conquering through faithfulness inverts all worldly expectation of military power; those "with Him" share His identity as called (κλητοί), chosen (ἐκλεκτοί), and faithful (πιστοί) — the marks of the redeemed community (Rom 8:28-30).

verses.flow: And he says to me, "The waters that you saw, where the harlot sits, are peoples and multitudes and nations and tongues.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: And the ten horns that you saw, and the beast — these shall hate the harlot and shall make her desolate and naked, and shall devour her flesh, and shall burn her up with fire.
For God put it into their hearts to do His purpose, and to act with one purpose, and to give their kingdom to the beast, until the words of God shall be fulfilled.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: This verse reveals the theological key to the entire chapter: God Himself (ὁ θεός) sovereignly directs the actions of the beast's allies by placing His purpose (γνώμη, gnōmē) into their hearts. Even the rebellion of hostile kings serves the divine decree. "Until the words of God shall be fulfilled" (ἄχρι τελεσθήσονται οἱ λόγοι τοῦ θεοῦ) affirms that all prophetic utterance reaches its appointed completion under God's sovereign governance (cf. Isa 46:10; Dan 4:35).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And the woman whom you saw is the great city, the one having kingship over the kings of the earth."
verses.note: GREEK: "The great city" (ἡ πόλις ἡ μεγάλη) is a recurring designation in Revelation (11:8; 16:19; 18:10), here conclusively identified with the woman. "Having kingship" (ἔχουσα βασιλείαν) — the present participle indicates ongoing imperial dominion over the kings of the earth at the time of the vision. HISTORICAL: In the first-century context, this description most naturally pointed to Rome, the singular city exercising sovereignty over all known kingdoms, though the typological application extends to every Babylon-like system of anti-God imperial power throughout history.

verses.flow: After these things I saw another angel descending out of heaven, having great authority, and the earth was illuminated from his glory.
verses.note: GREEK: The angel has "great authority" (exousian megalēn, ἐξουσίαν μεγάλην) and his glory (doxa, δόξα) illuminates the entire earth. The verb ephōtisthē (ἐφωτίσθη, from phōtizō, Strong's G5461) is aorist passive — the earth "was lit up" as a completed event. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This angel's radiance echoes the kabod (glory) theophanies of the OT (Ezek 43:2), suggesting a being of supreme rank in the heavenly council, reflecting delegated divine glory.

verses.flow: And he cried out with a mighty voice, saying, "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great, and she has become a dwelling-place of demons, and a prison of every unclean spirit, and a prison of every unclean bird, and a prison of every unclean and hated beast."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The double proclamation "Fallen, fallen is Babylon" directly echoes Isaiah 21:9 (LXX). The three-fold "prison" (phylakē, φυλακή, Strong's G5438) of unclean spirits, birds, and beasts recalls the desolation oracles of Isaiah 13:21-22 and 34:11-15, where ruined cities become haunts of demonic beings. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The katoikētērion daimoniōn ("dwelling-place of demons") indicates that Babylon becomes territory fully given over to hostile spiritual powers.

verses.flow: For from the wine of the wrath of her fornication all the nations have fallen, and the kings of the earth committed fornication with her, and the merchants of the earth were made rich from the power of her luxury.
verses.note: GREEK: Strēnos (στρῆνος, Strong's G4764) is a rare word meaning "wanton luxury" or "sensual excess" — it occurs only here and in v.7 in the NT. THEOLOGICAL: The triad of nations, kings, and merchants represents the totality of Babylon's corrupting influence: political, royal, and economic. The "wine of the wrath of her fornication" fuses images of idolatrous seduction and divine fury (cf. Rev 14:8; Jer 51:7).

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: And I heard another voice out of heaven, saying, "Come out of her, my people, that you share not in her sins, and that you receive not of her plagues;"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Come out of her, my people" echoes the call of separation found in Isaiah 48:20, Isaiah 52:11, and Jeremiah 51:45. The verb synkoinōnēsēte (συγκοινωνήσητε, "share together in") implies active co-participation. THEOLOGICAL: God's people are called to a decisive exodus from the world system before judgment falls — a pattern repeated from Lot leaving Sodom (Gen 19:12-14) to Israel leaving Egypt.

verses.flow: for her sins have been heaped up as far as heaven, and God has remembered her unrighteous deeds.
verses.note: GREEK: The verb ekollēthēsan (ἐκολλήθησαν, from kollaō, Strong's G2853) means literally "were glued together" or "clung to" — her sins have accumulated and adhered to one another, reaching up to heaven. TYPOLOGY: This directly recalls the Tower of Babel imagery (Gen 11:4) — human rebellion piled up toward heaven — and Jeremiah 51:9 where Babylon's judgment "reaches to heaven."

verses.flow: Render to her even as she herself rendered, and double the double according to her works; in the cup which she mixed, mix for her double.

As much as she glorified herself and lived in luxury, so much torment and mourning give to her, for in her heart she says, "I sit as a queen, and a widow I am not, and mourning I shall by no means see."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore in one day shall her plagues come — death and mourning and famine — and with fire she shall be utterly burned, for mighty is the Lord God who has judged her.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "mighty is the Lord God who has judged her" (ischyros kyrios ho theos ho krinas autēn) is a declaration of divine sovereignty over all earthly powers. The "one day" judgment contrasts with Babylon's claim of eternal queenship (v.7). TYPOLOGY: The plagues of death, mourning, and famine echo the plagues upon Egypt (Exod 7-12), confirming the Exodus typology of God's people delivered while the oppressor is destroyed.

verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the kings of the earth, who committed fornication and lived in luxury with her, shall weep and beat their breasts over her when they see the smoke of her burning,

verses.flow: standing from afar because of the fear of her torment, saying, "Woe, woe, the great city, Babylon the mighty city, for in one hour your judgment has come."
And the merchants of the earth weep and mourn over her, for no one buys their cargo anymore —

cargo of gold and silver and precious stone and pearls, and fine linen and purple and silk and scarlet, and every thyine wood and every vessel of ivory and every vessel of most precious wood and of bronze and of iron and of marble,
and cinnamon and spice and incense and ointment and frankincense, and wine and oil and fine flour and wheat, and cattle and sheep, and of horses and of chariots, and of bodies and souls of men.
verses.note: GREEK: The list climaxes with sōmatōn kai psychas anthrōpōn — literally "bodies and souls of men." Sōma (σῶμα, Strong's G4983) here refers to slave bodies, while psychas (ψυχάς) denotes the whole person. HISTORICAL: Roman commerce included a massive slave trade. verses.flow: And they cast dust upon their heads and cried out, weeping and mourning, saying, "Woe, woe, the great city, in which all who had ships on the sea were made rich from her costliness, for in one hour she has been made desolate."
Rejoice over her, O heaven, and you saints and apostles and prophets, for God has judged your judgment against her.
verses.note: GREEK: Ekrīnen ho theos to krima hymōn ex autēs — literally "God has judged your judgment out of her," meaning God has rendered against her the verdict that corresponds to what she did to you. THEOLOGICAL: This is the great reversal: heaven, saints, apostles, and prophets are summoned to rejoice because divine justice has been executed. The righteous are vindicated and the cry of the martyrs under the altar (Rev 6:9-10) is finally answered.

verses.flow: And one mighty angel took up a stone like a great millstone and cast it into the sea, saying, "Thus with violence shall Babylon the great city be thrown down, and shall by no means be found anymore."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The mighty angel's symbolic act of hurling the millstone into the sea directly echoes Jeremiah 51:63-64, where Seraiah is commanded to bind a stone to the scroll of Babylon's doom and cast it into the Euphrates. GREEK: Hormēmati (ὁρμήματι, Strong's G3731) — a NT hapax legomenon meaning "with violent rush" or "with impetus." The finality is absolute: ou mē heurethē eti, "shall by no means be found anymore."

verses.flow: And the sound of harpists and musicians and flute-players and trumpeters shall by no means be heard in you anymore, and every craftsman of every craft shall by no means be found in you anymore, and the sound of a millstone shall by no means be heard in you anymore,

and the light of a lamp shall by no means shine in you anymore, and the voice of bridegroom and bride shall by no means be heard in you anymore; for your merchants were the great ones of the earth, for by your sorcery all the nations were led astray.
verses.note: GREEK: Pharmakeía (φαρμακεία, Strong's G5331) encompasses sorcery, magical arts, and the use of drugs or potions in occult practice. THEOLOGICAL: Babylon's deception of all nations is ultimately spiritual — not merely economic or political but demonic enchantment. This identifies the world system's seductive power as fundamentally occult in nature. TYPOLOGY: The cessation of bridegroom and bride echoes Jeremiah 25:10 and 7:34, confirming Babylon as the antitype of condemned Jerusalem and Tyre alike.

Matt 23:35; Luke 11:50-51) and of "all who have been slain upon the earth" makes Babylon responsible for the totality of martyrdom throughout history. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This universal guilt suggests Babylon represents not merely one city or empire but the entire world system under the dominion of hostile spiritual powers arrayed against the Lamb and His people.

THEOLOGICAL: The heavenly multitude responds to the judgment of Babylon (Rev 18), affirming that God's judicial acts are themselves cause for worship.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Two grounds (ὅτι, hoti) are given for the praise: the truthfulness and righteousness of God's judgments, and the specific verdict against the great harlot. The avenging of the blood of servants echoes the cry of the martyrs under the altar (Rev 6:10) and fulfills the principle of Deut 32:43 (LXX). TYPOLOGY: The pattern of divine vindication of innocent blood runs from Abel (Gen 4:10) through the prophets (Luke 11:50-51) to the final reckoning here.

verses.note: GREEK: "Has begun to reign" (ἐβασίλευσεν, ebasíleusen) is an ingressive aorist, indicating the commencement of active, unchallenged reign. The full title "Lord God the Almighty" (κύριος ὁ θεός ὁ παντοκράτωρ) renders the LXX equivalent of YHWH Elohim Shaddai, the supreme divine council title. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This declaration parallels the seventh trumpet proclamation (Rev 11:15-17), where the cosmic transfer of sovereignty from rebellious powers to God and His Christ is announced.

verses.flow: Let us rejoice and exult and give the glory to Him, because the marriage of the Lamb has come, and His wife has made herself ready."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "marriage of the Lamb" (ὁ γάμος τοῦ ἀρνίου) consummates the bridal imagery that spans Scripture from the covenant at Sinai (Ezek 16:8-14), through the Song of Solomon, to Paul's mystery of Christ and the Church (Eph 5:31-32). The bride's self-preparation indicates both divine gifting (v. 8) and human responsive obedience. GREEK: ἀρνίον (arníon, Strong's G721) is the diminutive "little lamb," John's distinctive Christological title used 28 times in Revelation.

verses.flow: And it was given to her that she should clothe herself in fine linen, bright and clean; for the fine linen is the righteous deeds of the saints.
verses.note: GREEK: βύσσινον (býssinon, Strong's G1039) refers to fine linen of exceptional quality, associated with royalty and purity. The interpretive clause—"the fine linen is the righteous deeds (δικαιώματα, dikaiṓmata) of the saints"—is significant: dikaiṓmata denotes concrete acts of righteousness, not imputed status alone, showing the saints' faithful obedience has become their bridal garment.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The angel's refusal of worship (repeated in Rev 22:8-9) sharply distinguishes created beings from God. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Even exalted angelic beings within the heavenly court refuse worship, directing it solely to God—a polemic against angel veneration in Second Temple Judaism.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Three OT images converge: (1) the sharp sword from His mouth fulfills Isa 11:4 and Isa 49:2, where the Messiah strikes the earth with the word of His mouth; (2) shepherding with a rod of iron quotes Ps 2:9 (LXX), the royal Messianic psalm of enthronement; (3) the winepress of wrath draws on Isa 63:3 and Joel 3:13. verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The beast and false prophet—the satanic counterfeit of political and religious power—are cast alive (ζῶντες, zṓntes) into the lake of fire, a detail emphasizing conscious, unmediated judgment without the intervening step of death. HISTORICAL: The "lake of fire burning with brimstone" (θείῳ, theíō) evokes the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19:24) and draws on the imagery of Gehenna developed in Second Temple literature (1 Enoch 18:11-16; 21:7-10). This is the first mention of the lake of fire as the final destination of God's enemies.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "great supper of God" (v. 17) consuming the flesh of the nations inverts the marriage supper of the Lamb (v. 9): those not invited to the wedding feast become the feast of the birds. This gruesome tableau draws on Ezek 39:17-20, the eschatological feast after the defeat of Gog—a typological pattern John applies to the final battle. STRUCTURE: Revelation 19 thus presents two suppers in deliberate contrast: the marriage supper of life (v. 9) and the carrion supper of death (vv. 17-21), framing the ultimate binary of human destiny.

verses.note: HISTORICAL: Gog and Magog derive from Ezek 38-39, where Gog of the land of Magog leads a final eschatological assault against restored Israel. TYPOLOGY: The "four corners of the earth" signifies a universal, worldwide rebellion—a final recapitulation of every hostile nation rising against the Lord and his Anointed (Ps 2:1-3).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Death and Hades are here personified as cosmic powers that are themselves subjected to final judgment, being cast into the lake of fire. verses.flow: The foundations of the wall of the city were adorned with every precious stone: the first foundation, jasper; the second, sapphire; the third, chalcedony; the fourth, emerald;
the fifth, sardonyx; the sixth, sardius; the seventh, chrysolite; the eighth, beryl; the ninth, topaz; the tenth, chrysoprase; the eleventh, jacinth; the twelfth, amethyst.
And the twelve gates were twelve pearls; each one of the gates was of one pearl. And the street of the city was pure gold, as transparent glass.
And I saw no temple in it, for the Lord God the Almighty is its temple, and the Lamb.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The absence of a temple (naos, G3485 — the inner sanctuary) is stunning: in the old order, the temple mediated God's presence; now God Himself and the Lamb ARE the temple. The co-identification of "the Lord God the Almighty" (ho Kyrios ho Theos ho Pantokratōr) with "the Lamb" (to arnion) as jointly constituting the temple is a profound assertion of the Lamb's full divine status. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Unmediated divine presence replaces all cultic structures — the goal toward which all temple theology pointed.

verses.flow: And the city has no need of the sun nor of the moon that they should shine upon it, for the glory of God illuminated it, and the lamp of it is the Lamb.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The city needing neither sun nor moon fulfills Isaiah 60:19-20. The Lamb as the "lamp" (lychnos, G3088) of the city identifies Christ as the source of eschatological illumination. THEOLOGICAL: The glory (doxa) of God is the light-source; the Lamb is the lamp through which that glory radiates — a Christological statement of the highest order, distinguishing yet uniting Father and Son in the economy of divine radiance.

verses.flow: And the nations shall walk by means of its light, and the kings of the earth bring their glory into it.
And its gates shall in no way be shut by day, for night shall not be there.
And they shall bring the glory and the honor of the nations into it.

verses.flow: And there shall in no way enter into it anything common, nor one practicing abomination and falsehood, but only those written in the book of life of the Lamb.
verses.note: GREEK: "Common" (koinon, G2839) means ritually profane or defiled — the opposite of holy (hagios). THEOLOGICAL: Entry is determined solely by inscription in "the book of life of the Lamb" (to biblion tēs zōēs tou arniou) — salvation is by the Lamb's sovereign election and sacrificial redemption, not human merit. TYPOLOGY: The exclusion of the unclean from the holy city fulfills Isaiah 52:1 and Ezekiel 44:9, while the "book of life" motif reaches back to Exodus 32:32-33 and Daniel 12:1.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he showed me a river of water of life, bright as crystal, proceeding out of the throne of God and of the Lamb.
verses.note: GREEK: "Potamon" (ποταμόν, Strong's G4215) denotes a full river, not a stream. "Lampron hōs krystallon" (bright as crystal) echoes the divine radiance imagery. TYPOLOGY: This river echoes Eden's river (Gen 2:10) and Ezekiel's temple river (Ezek 47:1-12), now flowing from the unified throne of God and the Lamb — the eschatological fulfillment of paradise restored.

verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: In the midst of its street, and on this side and on that side of the river, was the tree of life, producing twelve fruits, yielding its fruit according to each month; and the leaves of the tree were for the healing of the nations.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "xylon zōēs" (ξύλον ζωῆς, tree of life) recalls both Genesis 2:9 and Ezekiel 47:12. Twelve fruits corresponding to twelve months suggests perpetual abundance for God's people. The healing of the nations (ethnōn) fulfills the Abrahamic promise that all nations would be blessed (Gen 12:3). GREEK: "Therapeia" (G2322) means healing or curative service, the root of English "therapy."

verses.flow: And every cursed thing shall be no more. And the throne of God and of the Lamb shall be in it, and His servants shall render sacred service to Him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Katathema" (κατάθεμα, G2652) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning "accursed thing" or "anything devoted to destruction." The curse of Genesis 3:17 is fully reversed. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The shared throne of God and the Lamb signifies the full co-regency of the Father and the Son; "latreuō" (G3000) denotes priestly, sacred service rendered exclusively to deity.

verses.flow: And they shall see His face, and His name shall be upon their foreheads.
And night shall be no more, and they have no need of the light of a lamp and the light of the sun, for the Lord God shall illuminate them, and they shall reign unto the ages of the ages.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The abolition of night and of all need for created light sources culminates the theme begun in Rev 21:23. "Kyrios ho theos" as the source of illumination echoes Isaiah 60:19-20. "Basileusousin eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn" — they shall reign unto the ages of the ages — confirms the eternal co-regency of the saints with God, fulfilling Daniel 7:18, 27.

verses.flow: And he said to me, "These words are faithful and true." And the Lord, the God of the spirits of the prophets, sent His angel to show to His servants the things which must come to pass in quickness.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "And behold, I am coming quickly. Blessed is the one keeping the words of the prophecy of this book."
verses.note: GREEK: "Erchomai tachy" (ἔρχομαι ταχύ) — "I am coming quickly." "Tachy" (G5035) denotes swiftness or suddenness rather than temporal nearness. This phrase appears three times in this chapter (vv. 7, 12, 20), forming a structural bracket. TYPOLOGY: "Makarios ho tērōn" (blessed is the one keeping) echoes the beatitude of Rev 1:3, forming an inclusio for the entire book.

verses.flow: And I, John, am the one hearing and seeing these things. And when I heard and saw, I fell down to worship before the feet of the angel who was showing me these things.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: And he says to me, "See that you do not! I am a fellow-servant of yours, and of your brothers the prophets, and of those keeping the words of this book. Worship God!"
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The angel's refusal of worship is theologically critical: angelic beings, though members of the divine council and heavenly court, are "syndouloi" (σύνδουλοι, G4889) — fellow-servants — not objects of worship. Worship (proskynēsis) belongs to God alone. This reinforces the distinction between the Creator and all created heavenly beings (cf. Rev 19:10; Col 2:18).

verses.flow: And he says to me, "Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this book, for the time is near."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "Let the one doing unrighteousness do unrighteousness still, and let the filthy one be made filthy still, and let the righteous one do righteousness still, and let the holy one be made holy still."
"Behold, I am coming quickly, and My reward is with Me, to render to each one as his work is."
"I am the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus claims the title "Alpha and Omega" — previously attributed to God the Father in Rev 1:8 and 21:6. Combined with "the First and the Last" (cf. Isa 44:6; 48:12, titles of YHWH) and "the Beginning and the End," this constitutes one of the strongest Christological identity claims in Scripture, asserting Christ's full deity and eternal nature.

verses.flow: Blessed are those who wash their robes, so that their authority shall be over the tree of life, and they may enter by the gates into the city.

verses.flow: Outside are the dogs and the sorcerers and the fornicators and the murderers and the idolaters, and everyone loving and practicing falsehood.
"I, Jesus, sent My angel to testify to you these things for the churches. I am the Root and the Offspring of David, the bright Morning Star."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "The Root and the Offspring (genos) of David" unites Christ's pre-existence (Root, cf. Isa 11:1, 10; Rom 15:12) with His incarnate Davidic lineage (Offspring). "The bright Morning Star" (ho astēr ho lampros ho prōinos) fulfills the prophecy of Numbers 24:17 and echoes Rev 2:28. GREEK: "Genos" (G1085) means race, stock, or offspring — emphasizing biological descent.

verses.flow: And the Spirit and the Bride say, "Come!" And let the one hearing say, "Come!" And let the one thirsting come; let the one desiring take the water of life freely.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Spirit (pneuma) and the Bride (nymphē) issue a united invitation, "Come!" — the church and the Holy Spirit call in one voice for both the return of Christ and the coming of the thirsty to partake. "Hydōr zōēs dōrean" (water of life freely) echoes Isaiah 55:1 and John 7:37-38. STRUCTURE: This verse transitions from prophetic revelation to gospel invitation, bridging eschatology and soteriology.

verses.flow: I testify to everyone hearing the words of the prophecy of this book: if anyone adds upon them, God shall add upon him the plagues having been written in this book.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: And if anyone takes away from the words of the book of this prophecy, God shall take away his part from the tree of life and out of the holy city, the things having been written in this book.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The One testifying these things says, "Yes, I am coming quickly." Amen! Come, Lord Jesus!
verses.note: GREEK: "Erchou kyrie Iēsou" (Ἔρχου, κύριε Ἰησοῦ) is the Greek equivalent of the Aramaic "Maranatha" (מרנא תא, 1 Cor 16:22) — "Come, Lord!" This is the final prayer of the canonical New Testament. THEOLOGICAL: That John addresses this prayer directly to Jesus as "Lord" (Kyrios) confirms early Christian worship of Christ as divine. The threefold "I am coming quickly" (vv. 7, 12, 20) receives its answering "Amen" here.

verses.flow: The grace of the Lord Jesus be with all.
